title: How The US Can Meet AI Energy Demand
gist: Gavekal argues conventional wisdom is wrong that the US AI buildout will be slowed primarily by electricity scarcity—greater efficiency in grid management and storage could meet much of new AI-driven electricity demand over the next five years without requiring new power plants, making the grid bottleneck a solvable software and optimization problem rather than purely a generation problem.

themes.theme: US AI Energy Constraint Is Overstated

insights.claim: Gavekal argues the conventional wisdom that the US AI buildout will be slowed by electricity scarcity is wrong—greater efficiency in grid management and storage could meet much of the new AI-driven demand over the next five years, without building new power plants.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: If Gavekal is right, the near-term investment opportunity is in grid optimization and storage software, not just generation capex—a different positioning than the prevailing 'build more power plants' consensus for AI infrastructure.
insights.quote: there is a lot the US can do to squeeze more juice out of the current infrastructure. Greater efficiency in grid management and storage could meet much of the new AI-driven demand over the next five years.
insights.locator: But aside from building new power plants

insights.claim: AI data centers are 'suddenly adding a lot of new electricity demand on a system that has not grown in two decades'—the constraint is connecting new data centers to the electric grid, not energy availability per se.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: The grid interconnection bottleneck—not power generation—is the binding constraint for US AI expansion; companies and investors focused only on power generation are solving the wrong problem.
insights.quote: AI data centers are suddenly adding a lot of new electricity demand on a system that has not grown in two decades
insights.locator: Solving the power constraint is indeed a problem
